summary: Gruber examines Google's evolving 'Material Design' language and its implications across platforms. He notes that while iOS, Android, and Windows Phone superficially converged on flatness, they're diverging in meaningful ways beneath the surface. Google's iOS apps increasingly feel like Android apps transplanted to iOS, ignoring platform conventions like swipe-to-go-back. He then pivots to the broader native-vs-web debate, observing that Google and Facebook have gone all-in on native apps for mobile, abandoning the web-app idealism of a few years prior. The irony is that Google's one major new web app, Inbox, only works in Chrome — undermining the cross-platform promise of web standards.
key_points: Google's Material Design creates a unified look across platforms but makes their iOS apps feel like Android apps running on iOS, ignoring iOS conventions
The superficial convergence toward 'flatness' obscures meaningful divergence in how iOS, Android, and Windows Phone are evolving their design languages
Google and Facebook have abandoned web-app idealism in favor of native apps for mobile, a major shift from the 2008-2011 era
Google Inbox launching as Chrome-only on desktop ironically undermines the cross-platform web-app dream that Google once championed
Write-once-run-everywhere remains a pipe dream because prioritizing user experience over developer convenience naturally favors native apps
money_quotes: Talking about flatness is simply too superficial to be a useful discussion.
To me, they look and feel like Android apps running on iOS.
The point of making apps shouldn't be about making life easier for developers, it's about making the best possible apps for users.
If you value user experience above developer convenience, it's easy to see why native apps are winning the war.

Practicality trumps idealism in the long-run, and the idea that the post-IE world of web browsers would lead to a world of universal cross-platform software is pretty much the definition of an idealistic crusade.
A Chrome-only app from Google — even if only temporary — is not how the world of standards-supporting web apps was supposed to work, in the aftermath of the breakup of Microsoft's IE hegemony.

Google is cultivating a single look-and-feel for their apps. But for mobile they're creating them as separate native Android and iOS apps. But their latest web app for desktop PCs, Inbox, only runs in one browser, Chrome.
key_insight: Google's embrace of native apps and platform-specific design, combined with shipping a Chrome-only web app, reveals that even the biggest proponents of the open web have conceded that practicality and user experience trump cross-platform idealism.
